slides.title: Range Returns 🔁
slides.content:
## Looping Map Entries

Remember the superpower **`range`** loop we used for slices? It works identically on maps!

Except, instead of giving you the `Index` and `Value`, it automatically hands you the **`Key`** and **`Value`**!

slides.title: Key, Value := range 🗺️
slides.content: Let's iterate through all the ages we securely saved.

quizData.question: When iterating over a slice, `range` gives you `index, value`. What does it give you when iterating over a map?
quizData.explanation: Because maps are strictly Key-Value pairs, `range` perfectly extracts the Key first, then the specific Value.

slides.title: A Chaotic Rule 🌪️
slides.content:
## Chaos by Design

Here is a bizarre (but intentional) aspect of Go: **Maps are unordered.**

When you use `range` over an Array or a Slice, the items print strictly from start to finish (0, 1, 2, 3).

When you iterate over a Map, Go actively randomizes the order! You can run the code 5 times and get 5 completely different printing orders. Never assume a map will stay in the order you typed it.

quizData.question: If you loop over a `map` in Go, the items will always print in the exact sequential order you securely added them.
quizData.explanation: False! Maps actively randomize iteration order in Go to prevent you from accidentally relying on 'order' when you shouldn't.
